explanation: SET NOCOUNT OFF leaves row-count messages enabled, adding unnecessary client and network traffic.
SET XACT_ABORT OFF can leave transactions partially applied after certain run-time errors.
SELECT * exposes all current and future columns and couples callers to the table schema.
TOP (10) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary set of rows.
OrderID is the primary key of Sales.Orders, so DISTINCT performs unnecessary duplicate elimination.
The NOLOCK hint permits dirty, missing, or duplicated reads.
Applying YEAR to OrderDate in the predicate prevents direct index matching and is non-sargable.
The int CustomerID column is compared with an nvarchar variable, potentially causing an implicit conversion.
The optional-parameter OR predicate can produce poor cardinality estimates and limit index seeking.
The LIKE pattern starts with %, preventing a normal seek on Comments.
Wrapping the indexed CustomerID column in ISNULL makes the predicate non-sargable.
Adding zero to the OrderID column prevents direct matching against the primary-key index.
The <> predicate can require scanning a broad portion of the CustomerID index or table.
CROSS JOIN multiplies every order by every customer and has no restricting join predicate.
TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary order/customer combination.
Comma-separated tables with a WHERE join condition use legacy join syntax and make accidental Cartesian joins easier.
TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary matching order.
The correlated scalar COUNT subquery performs a lookup for each outer order row.
The correlated EXISTS check is evaluated per outer row and may be better expressed as a set-based join or aggregation.
The same CASE expression is repeated in the SELECT list and ORDER BY clause.
ORDER BY 2 depends on the select-list position and is fragile when columns are reordered.
Pagination orders only by OrderDate and lacks a stable unique tie-breaker such as OrderID.
The branches select disjoint OrderID literals, so UNION performs unnecessary duplicate elimination.
COUNT(*) > 0 counts matching rows when an EXISTS predicate would stop after the first match.
SELECT assignment over multiple rows silently retains an arbitrary last value for @OrderID.
SELECT INTO implicitly creates #ImplicitOrders and its schema, reducing control over the temporary table definition.
TOP (5) without ORDER BY inserts an arbitrary subset of orders.
The cursor and FETCH pattern processes rows procedurally instead of using a set-based operation.
The cursor source uses TOP (2) without ORDER BY and therefore selects arbitrary rows.
The WHILE loop performs iterative row-style processing that should generally be replaced with a set-based operation.
The dynamic SQL text is built by concatenating @CustomerID instead of using a parameter.
EXEC executes dynamically constructed SQL without parameterized inputs.
The SQL string embeds @OrderID through string concatenation instead of binding it as a parameter.
sp_executesql is called without a parameter definition or parameter values.
#Scratch is explicitly created but is not dropped before the procedure ends.
The CATCH block only returns the error message and neither rethrows nor reports failure status.
RAISERROR is used for a new error path; THROW provides modern error propagation semantics.
PRINT is buffered and unsuitable for structured or reliably timed diagnostics.
The business identifier CustomerID = 1 is embedded directly instead of being supplied as a parameter.
The unlabelled date literal '20160101' embeds business logic directly in the predicate.
The string '01/02/2016' is converted to date without an explicit unambiguous format or style.
COALESCE is applied to both join columns, inhibiting normal join optimization and changing NULL matching semantics.
TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary matching order.
The self-join only retrieves the same OrderID already available from the outer Orders row.
TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary matching order.
Sorting by the nvarchar(MAX) Comments column can require expensive wide-row sorting.
SUM aggregates the entire Sales.OrderLines fact-like table without a restrictive filter.
The row-level OrderID > 0 predicate should be applied in WHERE before grouping.
The aggregate without GROUP BY returns one row, making ORDER BY OrderCount unnecessary.
MERGE has documented concurrency and correctness edge cases; separate update/insert logic is safer.
Comments = Comments writes the existing value, causing unnecessary logging and locking.
DELETE uses the non-key Quantity predicate and may remove many unrelated order lines.
WAITFOR DELAY blocks a worker thread and should generally be replaced with asynchronous scheduling.
BEGIN TRANSACTION is not enclosed in a TRY/CATCH error-handling boundary.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT ON changes session state and creates fragile identity-management coupling.
sp_who is invoked without a schema qualifier.
SET ROWCOUNT 1 changes session behavior and is not restored before procedure completion.
The dynamic SQL string concatenates @CustomerName into executable text.
User-supplied @CustomerName is concatenated directly into dynamic SQL, allowing SQL injection.
sp_executesql executes SQL containing user input without parameterized inputs.
sp_executesql is invoked without a schema qualifier.
UPDATE #DemoCustomers has no WHERE clause and modifies every row in the temporary table.
Filtering o.OrderDate in WHERE rejects NULL outer rows and effectively converts the LEFT JOIN to an INNER JOIN.
The unlabelled date literal '2016-01-01' embeds business logic directly in the query.
Comparing DeliveryMethodID with NULL using = never evaluates true; use IS NULL instead.
The scalar UDF dbo.GetCustomerType is invoked per selected customer and can force row-by-row evaluation.
The transaction CATCH block only selects the error message and neither rethrows nor returns failure status.
The CATCH block handling a transaction does not roll back before handling the error.
The hardcoded INDEX hint couples the query to a specific physical index and can become invalid or harmful.
The query contains three nested derived-table levels and should be refactored for readability and maintainability.
SELECT * from the table-valued function exposes all returned columns and couples callers to its result schema.
The multi-statement table-valued function can have poor cardinality estimates and should be replaced with an inline TVF where possible.
